title: The Seven Principles for Making Marriage Work
subtitle: John Gottman — a scientific approach to relationships and divorce prediction
description: A checklist based on the foundational work of psychology professor John Gottman, grounded in 40+ years of laboratory research with couples. Gottman can predict divorce with 94% accuracy after just 15 minutes of observing a couple's argument. In this book, he reveals what distinguishes stable, happy couples from those headed for divorce. We'll break down the 'Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse' in relationships, learn to build 'love maps,' manage conflict, and create shared meaning. This is not just advice, but a scientifically grounded system for building and maintaining intimate relationships.

items.title: Principle 1: Enhance Your Love Maps
items.source: John Gottman — The Seven Principles for Making Marriage Work, Chapter 3
items.text: A 'Love Map' is the cognitive space in the brain where all information about a partner is stored: their dreams, fears, preferences, current stresses, important people in their life. Gottman found that in happy couples, partners constantly update these maps, showing curiosity about each other's inner world. They know the spouse's favorite music, what's currently bothering them at work, what they dream about. This is not just 'being in the know,' but active curiosity about the partner's personality. In couples headed for divorce, love maps are outdated and superficial. Research shows: husbands who know the 'little things' about their wives (favorite color, best friend, current stress) have significantly higher marital satisfaction. Important: updating love maps is not interrogation, but showing sincere interest in everyday conversations. 'How was your day?' — a question that, with proper listening, replenishes the love map.
items.tip: Ask your partner one open-ended question daily about their inner world: 'What was the hardest part of your day?', 'What have you been thinking about on the way home?', 'Is there anything you've been dreaming about lately?' Listen without interrupting or offering solutions.

items.title: Principle 2: Nurture Your Fondness and Admiration
items.source: John Gottman — The Seven Principles for Making Marriage Work, Chapter 4
items.text: The second principle is maintaining the 'fondness and admiration system.' This is an antidote to contempt, the most destructive of the 'Horsemen.' Gottman discovered: in happy couples, partners consciously notice and verbally express what they value in each other. They focus on positive qualities, not faults. In couples headed for divorce, this system is atrophied: partners see only the bad in each other, interpret neutral actions negatively, and stop noticing the good. The key exercise: 'I appreciate...' — a daily or weekly expression of gratitude for specific actions or qualities of the partner. This is not just 'you're great,' but 'I appreciate that you made dinner even though you were tired yourself.' Gottman emphasizes: fondness and admiration are a choice, a habit that can be consciously cultivated, even when feelings seem to have cooled. Feelings often follow actions, not vice versa.
items.tip: Establish a ritual: every night before bed, tell your partner one thing you are grateful for that very day. Be specific: not 'you're good,' but 'thank you for listening to me when I was complaining about work.'

items.title: Principle 3: Turn Toward Each Other Instead of Away
items.source: John Gottman — The Seven Principles for Making Marriage Work, Chapter 5
items.text: Gottman introduced the concept of the 'emotional bank account.' Every interaction is either a 'deposit' or a 'withdrawal.' A 'bid for connection' is any attempt by the partner to attract your attention, establish contact. It can be verbal ('Look at that beautiful bird!') or non-verbal (a sigh, a touch). You have a choice: 'turn toward' (respond) or 'turn away' (ignore). Gottman's 'Love Lab' studies showed a staggering difference: couples who stayed together after 6 years responded to bids for connection 86% of the time. Couples who divorced responded only 33% of the time. Ignoring bids is a micro-rejection that accumulates and destroys intimacy. 'Turning toward' doesn't mean dropping everything and engaging. Sometimes just saying 'Mmm, yeah, beautiful,' without looking away from what you're doing, is enough. The main thing is to signal 'I hear you, I'm with you.'
items.tip: Over the next week, actively track your partner's 'bids for connection.' Consciously try to respond to every one, even the smallest. Just 'uh-huh,' a nod, a smile. Notice how this changes the atmosphere.

items.title: The Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse: Criticism, Contempt, Defensiveness, Stonewalling
items.source: John Gottman — The Seven Principles for Making Marriage Work, Chapter 2
items.text: Gottman identified four destructive communication patterns that predict divorce with high accuracy. 1) **Criticism**: attacking the personality, not the behavior. 'You're selfish' instead of 'I was upset you didn't take out the trash.' 2) **Contempt**: the most destructive horseman. Sarcasm, mockery, eye-rolling, name-calling. This is a stance of superiority that undermines the partner's self-esteem. Gottman considers contempt the primary predictor of divorce. 3) **Defensiveness**: counter-attacking or playing the victim. 'I'm not to blame, you are...' Escalates conflict, preventing genuine listening. 4) **Stonewalling**: emotional withdrawal, where the listener stops reacting. Usually occurs after the first three horsemen have exhausted the nervous system. It happens physiologically faster in men due to a higher stress response. Antidotes: for criticism — 'I-statements' and soft startup; for contempt — cultivating fondness and admiration; for defensiveness — taking responsibility; for stonewalling — physiological soothing and taking a break.
items.tip: When you feel yourself getting angry and about to make a contemptuous comment, pause. Ask yourself: 'What am I feeling underneath this anger? Pain? Fear? Disappointment?' Express that, rather than attacking.

items.title: Principle 4: Let Your Partner Influence You
items.source: John Gottman — The Seven Principles for Making Marriage Work, Chapter 6
items.text: The fourth principle is especially important for men (statistically, they are more likely to resist their wife's influence), but relevant for both genders. 'Letting influence happen' is a willingness to consider the partner's opinion, compromise, share power in the relationship. Gottman found that in 81% of cases, it is wives who raise 'difficult' topics for discussion. A husband who refuses to listen and change ('stonewalling') dramatically increases the chances of divorce. Conversely, husbands who accept their wives' influence have four times more stable and happy marriages. 'Letting influence happen' does not mean 'always agreeing.' It means genuinely considering the partner's viewpoint as valid and seeking solutions that meet both needs. This is a shift from 'me against you' to 'us against the problem.' In happy couples, decisions are made collaboratively, and each voice has weight.
items.tip: In your next argument, consciously say: 'Tell me more about why this is so important to you. I want to understand.' Don't interrupt. Repeat what you heard in your own words: 'Did I understand correctly that you're worried about X and Y?' This is the first step toward accepting influence.

items.title: Two Types of Conflicts: Solvable and Perpetual
items.source: John Gottman — The Seven Principles for Making Marriage Work, Chapter 8
items.text: Gottman made a revolutionary discovery: 69% of all marital conflicts are 'perpetual' — they have no final solution. They are rooted in fundamental differences in personalities, values, temperaments. For example, one partner is an extrovert and loves company, the other is an introvert and values peace and quiet. One is a spender, the other frugal. One wants children, the other hesitates. Happy couples differ from unhappy ones not in the absence of perpetual conflicts, but in their ability to manage them without damaging the relationship. In unhappy couples, people try to 'win' the perpetual conflict, change their partner, leading to escalation and alienation. In happy ones, they accept differences, respect each other's dreams, and create 'dialogue,' not 'debate.' The goal in a perpetual conflict is not to solve the problem, but to learn to live with it, maintaining humor, respect, and intimacy. Gottman suggests the technique of 'untangling': exploring the deeper dream or value behind each person's stance, and finding ways to partially satisfy both dreams.
items.tip: Identify one 'perpetual' conflict in your couple. Instead of trying to solve it, have a conversation where each tells what dream, value, or childhood experience lies behind their position. Listen without objections. The goal is to understand, not agree.

items.title: Principle 5: Solve Solvable Problems
items.source: John Gottman — The Seven Principles for Making Marriage Work, Chapter 9
items.text: For the 31% of 'solvable' conflicts, Gottman proposes a five-step model: 1) **Softened Startup**: start with an 'I-statement,' describing the situation without blame. 'Honey, I'd like to talk about... I'd appreciate...' instead of 'You again...'. 2) **Sending and Receiving Repair Attempts**: a 'repair attempt' is any action or phrase aimed at de-escalating tension: a joke, a touch, 'let's take a break', 'sorry'. Happy couples can send and receive such signals. 3) **Self-Soothing**: if your pulse is racing, take a break for at least 20 minutes for physiological calming. 4) **Compromise**: find a solution you can both live with. Not 'I win, you lose,' but 'we both get something and give something up.' 5) **Tolerance for Faults**: accept that your partner isn't perfect, nor are you. Some minor annoyances are easier to accept than to try to 'fix' the partner at the cost of domestic peace.
items.tip: Practice a 'softened startup.' Before raising a sensitive issue, pause and formulate the first sentence without 'you.' For example: 'I feel lonely when we don't have a heart-to-heart talk for a long time. I would like...'

items.title: Principle 6: Overcome Gridlock by Honoring Dreams
items.source: John Gottman — The Seven Principles for Making Marriage Work, Chapter 10
items.text: When a couple gets stuck in a 'perpetual' conflict, Gottman suggests moving to the level of 'the dream within the conflict.' Behind every rigid position lies a dream, hope, aspiration. For example, behind the position 'I want to move to the countryside' might lie a dream of peace, unity with nature, freedom from city bustle. Behind 'I want to stay in the city' — a dream of development, career, cultural life. The conflict seems unresolvable while arguing about geography. But if they share their dreams, a solution may satisfy both needs: for example, a house in the suburbs with fast city access, or weekend country house, or switching to remote work. The key is to stop arguing about the 'what' and start exploring the 'why.' Gottman suggests an exercise: each partner takes turns telling about their dream, and the other listens without criticizing or offering solutions, with the single goal of understanding. Often, just acknowledging and respecting the partner's dream ('I understand why this is so important to you') diffuses the conflict and opens the way to creative compromises.
items.tip: Choose one long-standing conflict. Ask your partner: 'What is most important to you behind this desire? What dream or value does it reflect?' Listen without interrupting. Then share your own dream.

items.title: Principle 7: Create Shared Meaning
items.source: John Gottman — The Seven Principles for Making Marriage Work, Chapter 11
items.text: The seventh principle is the peak of Gottman's pyramid. Happy couples do not just manage conflict and nurture fondness; they create a shared culture, rituals, meanings, and goals. 'Shared meaning' provides answers to: 'What are we together for?', 'What does our family symbolize?', 'What are our traditions?', 'What story are we creating?' It can be anything: joint volunteering, Sunday dinners with parents, a tradition of celebrating New Year in the mountains, shared values in raising children, spiritual practices. Creating shared meaning turns a couple from 'two people living together' into 'a team on a mission.' Gottman emphasizes the importance of 'rituals of connection' — regular, predictable actions that confirm 'we are a family.' It could be morning coffee together, an evening dog walk, a Friday candlelit dinner. In difficult times, these rituals and shared meanings hold the couple together, answering the question 'why are we going through this?'
items.tip: Create one new 'ritual of connection' for your couple. It should be something regular you do only together and that holds special meaning for you. For example, 'Saturday breakfast without phones,' 'evening walk before bed,' 'reading a book aloud together.'

items.title: The Secret of Happy Couples: Positive Sentiment Override and Repair
items.source: John Gottman — The Seven Principles for Making Marriage Work, Conclusion
items.text: Summarizing 40 years of research, Gottman highlights two global factors differentiating happy couples. The first is **Positive Sentiment Override** — a kind of 'rose-colored glasses' through which partners interpret each other's actions. In happy couples, a neutral action is perceived positively, and a negative one as an accident or resulting from tiredness. In unhappy couples, a neutral action is perceived as hostile, and a negative one as confirmation of 'bad character.' Positive sentiment override accumulates through 'deposits' in the emotional bank account (compliments, gratitude, responding to bids). The second factor is **Successful Repair**. All couples fight. The difference lies in whether they can 'repair' the relationship after a fight. Successful repair includes sincere apologies, taking responsibility, offering a compromise, humor, touch. Gottman emphasizes: repair doesn't have to be perfect; it has to be sincere and accepted. Couples who know how to make up only get stronger after conflicts.
items.tip: After your next fight, consciously make a 'repair.' It could be a simple 'I'm sorry we fought. I love you' or 'Let's hug and forget this nonsense.' Don't wait for the resentment to fade on its own — be the initiator of reconnection.

quiz.q: Which of the 'Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse' does Gottman consider the most destructive and the primary predictor of divorce?

a: Any attempt by a partner to gain attention and establish contact

quiz.q: What percentage of marital conflicts, according to Gottman, is 'perpetual' and has no final solution?
What is 'Positive Sentiment Override' in Gottman's terminology?
a: The tendency to see a partner's actions in the best light

quiz.q: What percentage of 'bids for connection' did couples who eventually divorce ignore (compared to 86% response in happy couples)?
a: They responded only 33% of the time
